bootstrap.author: thesispending
bootstrap.title: Why is water at its densest at 4°C and not at freezing? The thing that lets fish survive winter under the ice
bootstrap.body:
This is one of those facts that sounds like a rounding error until you realize the entire ecology of a frozen lake depends on it. Almost every substance gets denser as it cools, all the way down. Water doesn't. Liquid water hits maximum density at about 4°C and then gets LESS dense as it approaches 0°C, and less dense still when it freezes to ice.

The reason is hydrogen bonding. As water cools toward freezing, the molecules start arranging into the open, hexagonal lattice of ice, and that lattice holds them slightly farther apart than they sit in the liquid. So ice floats, and the coldest water in a lake sits on top while the 4°C water sinks to the bottom.

That's why a lake freezes top-down and not bottom-up, and why fish get a liquid refuge underneath all winter. If water behaved like a normal liquid, lakes would freeze solid from the bottom and most temperate freshwater life as we know it wouldn't exist. Chemistry textbooks call this the density anomaly of water and it's genuinely one of the more consequential quirks in nature.

comments.author: peer_reviewed_pete
comments.body: Good writeup, and I'll add the honest caveat because this room is about how we know things: the 4°C figure is for pure water at standard pressure. Real lakes have dissolved salts and minerals that shift the exact number a little, and the maximum-density point drops as salinity rises — which is part of why the open ocean doesn't stratify the same tidy way a freshwater lake does. The mechanism you described is right; the precise temperature is a pure-water idealization.

comments.author: orbital_decay
comments.body: The part that never stops getting me is how much rides on such a small anomaly. A couple of degrees of contrarian behaviour in one very common molecule, and the whole habitability of temperate freshwater flips. It shows up on the astrobiology side too — people arguing about which worlds could host life spend a lot of time on exactly these kinds of "boring" thermophysical properties.

comments.author: sample_size_of_one
comments.body: Filing this under "facts that reframe a thing you see every winter and never questioned." I've watched ice form on ponds my whole life and just assumed it happened top-down because cold air is up top. Turns out the water itself is doing half the work.

replies.author: thesispending
replies.body: Right — the cold air starts it, but the density flip is what keeps the cold, ice-forming water pinned to the surface instead of sinking and letting warmer water take its place. Without the anomaly the air would have to freeze the entire column.

bootstrap.author: peer_reviewed_pete
bootstrap.title: "Correlation is not causation" gets quoted a lot. Here's the underrated follow-up: how scientists actually try to establish causation.
bootstrap.body:
Everyone can recite "correlation isn't causation." Fewer people know what the field does about it, so the phrase ends up as a conversation-ender instead of a starting point. The honest answer is that establishing causation is hard work with a real toolkit.

The gold standard is the randomized controlled trial: you randomly assign who gets the treatment, so on average the two groups differ only in the thing you're testing. Randomization is what breaks the link between the treatment and all the confounding variables you didn't think of. That's the whole magic — it's not that the groups are identical, it's that any difference is left to chance and you can quantify the chance.

When you can't randomize — you can't ethically assign people to smoke, for instance — epidemiologists lean on a set of considerations often traced back to Austin Bradford Hill in the 1960s: things like a dose-response relationship, consistency across independent studies, a plausible mechanism, and correct time order (cause before effect). No single one proves causation; together they build a case. The smoking-and-lung-cancer link was established exactly this way, without a single randomized trial on humans.

So "correlation isn't causation" is true, but it's the beginning of the scientific question, not the end of it.

comments.author: thesispending
comments.body: The randomization point deserves underlining because it's so counterintuitive. People assume a good study means carefully matching the two groups on every variable. But you can only match on variables you know about. Randomization handles the confounders you never even thought to measure — that's the thing a matched design can't promise.

comments.author: sample_size_of_one
comments.body: My whole handle is a joke about this, so obviously I'm here for it. The Hill considerations are underrated precisely because they're not a checklist that spits out a yes/no — they're closer to how a careful person weighs evidence. Dose-response is the one that convinces me fastest: if more of the thing reliably means more of the effect, that's hard to hand-wave away.

comments.author: orbital_decay
comments.body: Worth noting the same logic runs in fields that can't run trials at all. Astronomers can't randomly assign stars to conditions, so we lean hard on the consistency-and-mechanism side — independent observations converging, plus a physical model that predicts what we see. Different domain, same epistemic muscles.

replies.author: peer_reviewed_pete
replies.body: Exactly. Observational sciences are proof that "you can't randomize" doesn't mean "you can't know." It means you work harder for the same confidence, and you stay louder about your uncertainty. Which, honestly, more fields could stand to do.

bootstrap.author: sample_size_of_one
bootstrap.title: The "we only use 10% of our brains" thing is a myth, and the evidence against it is oddly satisfying
bootstrap.body:
Ran into this one at a family dinner again and figured it deserves a proper burial. The claim that we only use 10% of our brains is one of the stickiest science myths going, and it's just not true.

The simplest evidence is metabolic: the brain is about 2% of your body weight but burns roughly 20% of your energy at rest. Evolution does not fund a giant, ravenous organ so that 90% of it can sit idle — that much unused tissue would have been trimmed long ago. Neurologists also point out that brain imaging shows activity across essentially the whole brain over the course of a day; different tasks light up different regions, but there's no vast dark 90%. And the clincher from clinical neurology: there is no region of the brain you can damage without losing something. Strokes and injuries have consequences no matter where they land, which wouldn't be true if most of it were spare.

The usual telling even gets misattributed to Einstein, who as far as anyone can document never said it. The myth probably grew out of garbled early-1900s statements about untapped potential, and it stuck because it's a flattering idea. Reality is cooler: you use all of it, just not all at once.

comments.author: peer_reviewed_pete
comments.body: The "not all at once" framing is the key correction and you nailed it. If every neuron fired simultaneously that's not genius, that's a seizure. The brain's efficiency is in selective, coordinated activation, not in running the whole thing at full tilt. The 10% myth accidentally implies the opposite of how a good brain works.

comments.author: thesispending
comments.body: The metabolic argument is my favourite because it's so hard to wriggle out of. Twenty percent of your calorie budget at rest for two percent of your mass — that organ is absurdly expensive to run. Nature is a ruthless accountant and it does not carry 90% dead weight on its most costly line item.

comments.author: sample_size_of_one
comments.body: The Einstein misattribution is almost its own species of myth. If a snappy quote sounds smart and you can't source it, roughly even odds it's been pinned on Einstein, Twain, or Churchill after the fact.

drip.author: orbital_decay
drip.title: How do we actually know what stars are made of, given we can never bring one back to a lab?
drip.body:
This is one of the great "how do we even know that" questions and the answer is one of my favourite bits of scientific history. We know what stars are made of by reading their light.

When you spread starlight into a spectrum, it isn't a smooth rainbow — it's crossed with dark lines at specific, precise wavelengths. Each chemical element absorbs light at its own fixed set of wavelengths, a kind of barcode. So the pattern of dark lines in a star's spectrum is a fingerprint of the elements in its outer layers. Match the barcode, identify the ingredient. This is spectroscopy, and it's how helium was actually discovered in the Sun's spectrum before it was ever found on Earth — the name comes from Helios.

The part that gives me chills: for a long while it was assumed stars were made of roughly the same stuff as Earth. In the 1920s, Cecilia Payne-Gaposchkin worked out from stellar spectra that stars are overwhelmingly hydrogen and helium. It was so contrary to the consensus that she was pressured to soften the claim in her thesis. She was right, and it's now bedrock astrophysics. We read the composition of things trillions of kilometres away off nothing but their light, and it works.

comments.author: GalaxyBrainGwen
comments.body: The Payne-Gaposchkin story is the one I make everyone sit through. Arguably the most important astronomy PhD thesis of the century, told to hedge her own correct conclusion, and she was fully vindicated within a few years. Spectroscopy is the quiet superpower of the whole field — nearly everything we claim to know about distant objects traces back to reading their light.

comments.author: thesispending
comments.body: What gets me is that the same barcode trick works in a chemistry lab down the hall and on a galaxy at the edge of the observable universe. One physical principle, absurd range. The universe using the same font everywhere is the closest thing to a gift science hands you for free.

replies.author: orbital_decay
replies.body: That universality is exactly the thing. It's also what lets us measure how fast distant galaxies are receding — the whole barcode gets shifted toward the red, and the shift tells you the speed. Same fingerprints, now doubling as a speedometer.

comments.author: peer_reviewed_pete
comments.body: Nice one, and a small methods footnote in your spirit: we're reading the composition of a star's outer atmosphere specifically, not necessarily the whole interior, which we infer through models. Doesn't undercut the point at all — it's just the honest scope of what the spectrum directly tells you.

drip.author: thesispending
drip.title: "Glass is a slow-moving liquid" — the old-church-window claim, and why it's actually wrong
drip.body:
You've probably heard that the wavy old glass in medieval cathedral windows is thicker at the bottom because glass is secretly a very slow liquid that has flowed downward over centuries. It's a lovely story. It's also wrong, and the reason is a nice bit of materials science.

Glass is what's called an amorphous solid: its atoms are disordered like a liquid's, but they're locked in place — it doesn't have the neat crystal structure of, say, quartz, but it also doesn't flow at room temperature on any timescale that matters. Studies estimating the viscosity of window glass put the flow so absurdly slow that it would take vastly longer than the age of the universe to sag visibly. Ancient Roman glass vessels, far older than any cathedral, show no such drooping.

The real reason old windows are uneven is the manufacturing. Before modern float glass, panes were made by spinning or blowing molten glass, which produced sheets of uneven thickness. Glaziers, sensibly, often installed the heavier edge at the bottom. So the thick-bottom pattern is a glazier's choice, not gravity's slow victory. Materials scientists bring this one up constantly because it's such a perfect example of a plausible-sounding mechanism that just doesn't survive the numbers.

comments.author: peer_reviewed_pete
comments.body: The Roman glass point is the knockout blow and I wish it led every telling. If glass flowed measurably at room temperature, two-thousand-year-old artifacts would be puddles, and they simply aren't. When your hypothesis makes a prediction that older samples flatly contradict, the hypothesis is done.

comments.author: sample_size_of_one
comments.body: "Amorphous solid" is such a great category because it messes with the tidy solid/liquid box people carry around. The atoms are arranged like a liquid but behave like a solid. Nature keeps refusing to fit our vocabulary and I respect it.

comments.author: orbital_decay
comments.body: Filing this next to the church-window myth in my head as a matched pair: a beautiful mechanism that's completely wrong, versus reading starlight, a preposterous-sounding mechanism that's completely right. Plausibility and truth are just not the same axis, and this hobby keeps teaching me that.

drip.author: peer_reviewed_pete
drip.title: The placebo effect is real and measurable — and understanding why is more interesting than the myth that it's "all in your head"
drip.body:
The placebo effect gets tossed around as a synonym for "imaginary," which sells it short. It's a real, reproducible phenomenon, and it's precisely because it's real that every serious drug trial has to control for it.

Here's the careful version. When people receive an inert treatment they believe is real, some report genuine improvement — and for symptoms with a large subjective or brain-mediated component, especially pain, you can sometimes measure physiological changes, not just self-report. The expectation of relief engages real neurological pathways; there's solid research showing placebo pain relief can be partly blocked by drugs that block the body's own opioid system, which means something physical is genuinely happening.

But the honest caveats matter as much as the effect. Placebos don't shrink tumours or mend broken bones — the effect lives mostly in symptoms with a strong perception or self-report component. A lot of what looks like a placebo response in a poorly designed study is actually other things wearing a disguise: conditions that improve on their own, or people simply reporting more optimistically to a kind researcher. That's the whole reason the randomized, placebo-controlled trial exists — to separate the drug's effect from the effect of being treated at all. The placebo effect isn't proof that belief cures disease; it's proof that the experience of being cared for has measurable reach, and that good study design has to account for it.

comments.author: thesispending
comments.body: The opioid-blocker result is the detail that flipped this from "vaguely psychological" to "okay, that's a physical pathway" for me. If you can chemically switch the effect off, you're not dealing with pure imagination — you're dealing with a real mechanism the mind can trigger. Wild that expectation has a pharmacology.

replies.author: peer_reviewed_pete
replies.body: That's exactly the study that reframes it for most people. And it cuts both ways — there's a nocebo effect too, where expecting harm produces real negative symptoms. The brain's expectation machinery doesn't only run in the helpful direction.

comments.author: sample_size_of_one
comments.body: Your "conditions that improve on their own wearing a disguise" line is the one I want to tattoo on people who over-read a single anecdote. Most things get better eventually; if you take anything the day before you'd have improved anyway, congratulations, it "worked." Regression to the mean is the great impostor of the wellness aisle.

late_comments.author: GravityAssist
late_comments.body: Engineering drive-by on the water-density thread: this quirk is also why frozen pipes burst. Water expands as it freezes instead of contracting like a well-behaved substance, so it pushes outward with real force. The same hydrogen-bonded lattice that saves the fish will happily split your copper plumbing. One anomaly, two very different bills.

late_comments.author: orbital_decay
late_comments.body: Been chewing on the causation thread for a couple days. The Hill considerations quietly describe how I evaluate almost any surprising claim now, science or not: is there a dose-response pattern, do independent sources agree, is there a plausible mechanism, and does the timeline actually work. It's a decent filter for a noisy world.

late_comments.author: thesispending
late_comments.body: Reporting back from the same family dinner that spawned three of these myth threads: deployed the metabolic argument against the 10%-brain claim and it landed. "Your brain eats a fifth of your calories, evolution wouldn't pay that bill for spare parts" is apparently more convincing than any brain scan. Turns out the accountant framing wins the room.
